source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 14.3-14.3.1 rpm install (pp. 256-257)

questions.q: Install the package file tree-1.7.0-15.el8.x86_64.rpm, showing a progress bar and progress info.
questions.why: -i installs, -v is verbose, -h shows the hash progress bar; installing a new package uses the Full Package Name.

questions.q: What do the -h and -v options add to an rpm operation?
options: -h shows a progress bar (hashes); -v gives verbose progress info
questions.why: -h prints a hash-mark progress bar and -v reports progress verbosely.

questions.q: Upgrade an already-installed package using the file wget-1.19.5-12.el8_10.x86_64.rpm (with progress bar).
questions.why: -U updates an installed package (and installs it if absent); -vh adds verbose + progress bar.
-e (erase) uninstalls; note it takes the Package Name, not the full file name.

questions.q: When do you use the Full Package Name versus the Package Name with rpm?
options: Full Package Name for a not-yet-installed package (e.g. install); Package Name for an already-installed one
questions.why: Installing a new package uses the full file name; operating on an installed package uses the Package Name (rpm reads /var/lib/rpm).

questions.q: Where does rpm store information about installed packages?
questions.why: The RPM database lives in /var/lib/rpm/, which is why installed packages can be referenced by short name.

questions.q: How does rpm handle dependencies, and what happens if one is missing?
options: It does NOT resolve them automatically; a missing dependency gives a 'failed dependencies' error you must fix manually
It downloads and installs dependencies for you
questions.why: rpm is a local manager, so you resolve dependencies yourself (e.g. rpm -ivh a.rpm b.rpm c.rpm); dnf is what resolves them automatically.
